prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
Imagine a sentence as an array of tokens. Each token is turned into a vector. For every token, the model makes three projections: Query (what am I looking for?), Key (what do I match with?), and Value (what information do I share?). To process one token, it takes that token’s Query and computes dot products with every token’s Key to get relevance scores. A softmax turns these scores into weights that sum to 1. The model then takes a weighted sum of the Value vectors. That result is the token’s context-aware representation—it “attends” more to relevant tokens, anywhere in the sequence.

Multi-head attention repeats this in parallel with several different projections (heads). Each head can focus on different patterns (e.g., nearby syntax vs. long-range references). Their outputs are concatenated and linearly mixed.

Attention works over all tokens at once, so it parallelizes well on GPUs, unlike RNNs. In decoders, a mask prevents looking at future tokens to keep generation causal. In encoder–decoder setups, cross-attention lets the decoder attend to the encoded input.

Stacking attention with small feed-forward networks builds deep, global understanding. That’s the core of Transformers: flexible, parallel, long-range context modeling.
